verses.text: Manasseh was twelve years old when he began to reign, and reigned fifty and five years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Hephzibah. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, after the abominations of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out before the children of Israel. Guidance: Manasseh — the longest and worst reign in Judah's history. The son born in Hezekiah's added years now takes the throne at twelve and reigns fifty-five years — the longest reign of any Judean king, and the most catastrophically evil. The verdict is unsparing: 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, after the abominations of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out before the children of Israel.' Manasseh does not merely tolerate sin; he actively imports the full catalog of Canaanite abomination — the very practices for which the LORD had driven out the original inhabitants. The chapter is showing us the most tragic of reversals: the son of Judah's greatest reformer becomes Judah's greatest corrupter. Everything Hezekiah trusted and built, Manasseh attacks; everything Hezekiah purged, Manasseh restores. The chapter is teaching us, with painful force, that godliness does not pass automatically from father to son — a Hezekiah can raise a Manasseh. The chapter is also teaching us a sobering lesson about the length of an evil reign: the LORD in His patience allowed this wicked king fifty-five years, the longest tenure in Judah's history, during which the rot spread so deep that even the later great reformation under Josiah could not avert the exile (the LORD specifically names Manasseh's sins as the cause Judah could not escape, 24:3-4). The chapter is teaching us that the long permission of evil is not the LORD's approval but His patience — and that the damage a sustained, determined apostasy does to a nation can outlast the apostate himself and seal the fate of generations. (2 Chr 33 records that Manasseh, taken captive to Babylon late in life, repented and was restored — a genuine personal conversion; but Kings, tracing the national consequences, records the damage his reign did, damage his personal repentance could not undo.)

For he built up again the high places which Hezekiah his father had destroyed; and he reared up altars for Baal, and made a grove, as did Ahab king of Israel; and worshipped all the host of heaven, and served them. And he built altars in the house of the LORD, of which the LORD said, In Jerusalem will I put my name. And he built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD. And he made his son pass through the fire, and observed times, and used enchantments, and dealt with familiar spirits and wizards: he wrought much wickedness in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger. Guidance: The catalog of Manasseh's abominations. The narrator lists Manasseh's sins in a sweeping inventory of evil, and each item is a deliberate reversal of his father's reform or a new depth of corruption. He 'built up again the high places which Hezekiah his father had destroyed' — undoing the signature reform. He 'reared up altars for Baal, and made a grove [Asherah], as did Ahab' — importing the very Baal-Asherah cult that doomed the northern Omrides into Judah. He 'worshipped all the host of heaven' — astral worship (sun, moon, stars), a Mesopotamian import. Worst of all, he 'built altars in the house of the LORD' — and 'for all the host of heaven in the two courts' — defiling the very temple of which God had said 'In Jerusalem will I put my name,' planting pagan altars in God's own house. And he 'made his son pass through the fire' (child sacrifice, like his grandfather Ahaz), 'observed times' (astrology/omens), 'used enchantments' (sorcery), and 'dealt with familiar spirits and wizards' (necromancy and mediums — explicitly forbidden, Lev 19:31, Deut 18:10-12). The summary: 'he wrought much wickedness in the sight of the LORD, to provoke him to anger.' The chapter is showing us apostasy at its most comprehensive — every category of forbidden worship and occult practice, climaxing in the defilement of the temple itself. The chapter is teaching us that determined evil does not merely neglect true worship but actively wars against it — Manasseh systematically rebuilt what Hezekiah destroyed and planted idolatry in the holiest place. The chapter is teaching us the special gravity of corrupting the place of worship: to set pagan altars in the house where God put His name is to assault the LORD at the very heart of the covenant. The chapter is teaching us that the phrase 'to provoke him to anger' names the deepest character of all this — it is not merely cultural drift but a deliberate offense against the living God, an accumulation of provocations that will, in time, bring down the judgment the rest of the chapter announces.

And he set a graven image of the grove that he had made in the house, of which the LORD said to David, and to Solomon his son, In this house, and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all tribes of Israel, will I put my name for ever: Neither will I make the feet of Israel move any more out of the land which I gave their fathers; only if they will observe to do according to all that I have commanded them, and according to all the law that my servant Moses commanded them. But they hearkened not: and Manasseh seduced them to do more evil than did the nations whom the LORD destroyed before the children of Israel. Guidance: The idol in the temple and the squandered promise. Manasseh's crowning blasphemy: 'he set a graven image of the grove [a carved Asherah idol] that he had made in the house' — he placed a pagan idol inside the temple itself. The narrator pointedly recalls what the LORD had said of this house to David and Solomon: 'In this house, and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all tribes of Israel, will I put my name for ever.' This was the place of God's chosen presence, the focus of all true worship; Manasseh filled it with an idol. The narrator also recalls the conditional promise that went with it: 'Neither will I make the feet of Israel move any more out of the land... ONLY IF they will observe to do according to all... the law that my servant Moses commanded.' The promise of secure dwelling in the land was conditioned on obedience. Then the verdict: 'But they hearkened not: and Manasseh seduced them to do more evil than did the nations whom the LORD destroyed.' Manasseh did not merely sin himself; he 'seduced' the whole nation into sin worse than the Canaanites whom God had judged. The chapter is showing us the squandering of an immense privilege. Judah held the chosen temple, the promise of perpetual dwelling, and the law of Moses — and Manasseh led them to forfeit it all by leading them past even the wickedness of the nations God had already destroyed. The chapter is teaching us the terrible power of a leader to corrupt those he leads: 'Manasseh SEDUCED them' — his evil was not contained to himself but pulled the whole people down. A wicked leader who 'makes the people to sin' bears a double guilt — for his own evil and for the multitude he drags after him. The chapter is teaching us the conditionality of the privilege of God's presence: the temple, the land, and the promises were never licenses for sin but were held under the covenant condition of obedience, and a nation that fills God's house with idols and out-sins the pagans cannot presume on the promise while trampling the condition. The chapter is teaching us that the highest privileges become the heaviest guilt when they are despised — to be the people of the chosen temple and then to set an idol in it is to invite the very judgment the next verses pronounce.

And the LORD spake by his servants the prophets, saying, Because Manasseh king of Judah hath done these abominations, and hath done wickedly above all that the Amorites did, which were before him, and hath made Judah also to sin with his idols: Therefore thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Behold, I am bringing such evil upon Jerusalem and Judah, that whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle. And I will stretch over Jerusalem the line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab: and I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down. And I will forsake the remnant of mine inheritance, and deliver them into the hand of their enemies; and they shall become a prey and a spoil to all their enemies; Because they have done that which was evil in my sight, and have provoked me to anger, since the day their fathers came forth out of Egypt, even unto this day. Guidance: The sentence of doom on Judah. The LORD speaks through His prophets a verdict whose horror is captured in unforgettable images. The charge: Manasseh 'hath done wickedly above all that the Amorites did' (worse than the Canaanites God had destroyed) and 'hath made Judah also to sin.' The sentence: 'Behold, I am bringing such evil upon Jerusalem and Judah, that whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle' — a disaster so appalling that the mere report of it will make ears ring. Two terrifying images follow. First: 'I will stretch over Jerusalem the line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab' — God will measure Jerusalem with the same measuring-line He used for Samaria's destruction and the same plumb-line by which He condemned Ahab's house. Judah will be judged by the standard that destroyed the apostate north; the south, having become like the north, will share the north's fate. Second: 'I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down' — a homely, devastating image of total emptying. God will scour Jerusalem clean of its inhabitants like a man wipes a plate and turns it over to drain, leaving nothing. He will 'forsake the remnant of mine inheritance, and deliver them into the hand of their enemies.' The reason: persistent provocation 'since the day their fathers came forth out of Egypt, even unto this day' — the whole long history of rebellion has come to its reckoning. The chapter is showing us the moment the exile of JUDAH becomes irrevocable. The northern kingdom fell in chapter 17; now the LORD pronounces the same doom on the south. Manasseh's reign is the tipping point — the LORD will later say that even Josiah's great reformation could not turn away this wrath 'because of all the provocations that Manasseh had provoked him withal' (23:26). The chapter is teaching us that there is a point at which accumulated, determined, nation-corrupting sin makes judgment certain. The LORD's patience is vast (He bore with Judah for centuries), but it is not infinite; Manasseh's sustained evil and seduction of the nation crossed a line. The chapter is teaching us the sobering truth that a leader's sin can have irreversible national consequences — Manasseh would personally repent (2 Chr 33), but the damage to the nation was done, and the sentence stood. The 'ears shall tingle' and the 'wiped dish' are the LORD's own words for how complete and shocking the coming destruction would be.

And the LORD spake by his servants the prophets, saying, Because Manasseh king of Judah hath done these abominations, and hath done wickedly above all that the Amorites did, which were before him, and hath made Judah also to sin with his idols: Therefore thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Behold, I am bringing such evil upon Jerusalem and Judah, that whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle. And I will stretch over Jerusalem the line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab: and I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down. And I will forsake the remnant of mine inheritance, and deliver them into the hand of their enemies; and they shall become a prey and a spoil to all their enemies; Because they have done that which was evil in my sight, and have provoked me to anger, since the day their fathers came forth out of Egypt, even unto this day. Guidance: The sentence of doom on Judah. The LORD speaks through His prophets a verdict whose horror is captured in unforgettable images. The charge: Manasseh 'hath done wickedly above all that the Amorites did' (worse than the Canaanites God had destroyed) and 'hath made Judah also to sin.' The sentence: 'Behold, I am bringing such evil upon Jerusalem and Judah, that whosoever heareth of it, both his ears shall tingle' — a disaster so appalling that the mere report of it will make ears ring. Two terrifying images follow. First: 'I will stretch over Jerusalem the line of Samaria, and the plummet of the house of Ahab' — God will measure Jerusalem with the same measuring-line He used for Samaria's destruction and the same plumb-line by which He condemned Ahab's house. Judah will be judged by the standard that destroyed the apostate north; the south, having become like the north, will share the north's fate. Second: 'I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish, wiping it, and turning it upside down' — a homely, devastating image of total emptying. God will scour Jerusalem clean of its inhabitants like a man wipes a plate and turns it over to drain, leaving nothing. He will 'forsake the remnant of mine inheritance, and deliver them into the hand of their enemies.' The reason: persistent provocation 'since the day their fathers came forth out of Egypt, even unto this day' — the whole long history of rebellion has come to its reckoning. The chapter is showing us the moment the exile of JUDAH becomes irrevocable. The northern kingdom fell in chapter 17; now the LORD pronounces the same doom on the south. Manasseh's reign is the tipping point — the LORD will later say that even Josiah's great reformation could not turn away this wrath 'because of all the provocations that Manasseh had provoked him withal' (23:26). The chapter is teaching us that there is a point at which accumulated, determined, nation-corrupting sin makes judgment certain. The LORD's patience is vast (He bore with Judah for centuries), but it is not infinite; Manasseh's sustained evil and seduction of the nation crossed a line. The chapter is teaching us the sobering truth that a leader's sin can have irreversible national consequences — Manasseh would personally repent (2 Chr 33), but the damage to the nation was done, and the sentence stood. The 'ears shall tingle' and the 'wiped dish' are the LORD's own words for how complete and shocking the coming destruction would be.

Judah will be judged by the standard that destroyed the apostate north; the south, having become like the north, will share the north's fate. The LORD's patience is vast (He bore with Judah for centuries), but it is not infinite; Manasseh's sustained evil and seduction of the nation crossed a line. Moreover Manasseh shed innocent blood very much, till he had filled Jerusalem from one end to another; beside his sin wherewith he made Judah to sin, in doing that which was evil in the sight of the LORD. Guidance: The innocent blood. Beyond all his idolatry, the narrator adds one more terrible charge: 'Manasseh shed innocent blood very much, till he had filled Jerusalem from one end to another.' His reign was not only spiritually corrupt but murderously violent — he flooded the capital with the blood of the innocent. (Jewish tradition holds that the prophet Isaiah was among his victims, executed by being sawn in two — the likely reference behind Heb 11:37, 'they were sawn asunder'; Manasseh certainly slaughtered those who resisted his apostasy.) This bloodshed is named as a distinct and aggravating sin, 'beside his sin wherewith he made Judah to sin.' The chapter is showing us that idolatry and injustice travel together — the king who filled God's house with idols also filled God's city with innocent blood. False worship and the shedding of innocent blood are repeatedly linked in Scripture, because a heart that abandons the true God loses the restraint that protects human life (and because Manasseh murdered those who opposed his idolatry). The chapter is teaching us that the LORD takes special account of innocent blood. This sin in particular is later named as the unforgivable national stain: 'the LORD would not pardon... for the innocent blood that he shed: for he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood' (24:4). The chapter is teaching us that the murder of the innocent — especially of the faithful who resist evil — is among the gravest of all sins in God's sight, a crime whose stain He will require. The chapter is teaching us that the deepest measure of a society's evil is not only its false worship but its treatment of the innocent and the righteous; Manasseh's Jerusalem, full of idols and full of innocent blood, had become everything the covenant was meant to prevent.

Now the rest of the acts of Manasseh, and all that he did, and his sin that he sinned, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And Manasseh slept with his fathers, and was buried in the garden of his own house, in the garden of Uzza: and Amon his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Manasseh's close. The standard formula closes the longest and darkest reign, with the unusual note that 'his sin that he sinned' is recorded — even the regnal summary cannot omit his defining mark. He is buried not in the royal tombs but 'in the garden of his own house, in the garden of Uzza' (a departure from the usual burial 'with his fathers in the city of David,' perhaps reflecting the dishonor of his reign, or simply a change in royal burial practice). Amon his son succeeds. The chapter is closing the account of the man whose reign sealed Judah's doom. (2 Chronicles 33 records the remarkable epilogue that Kings omits: Manasseh was taken in chains to Babylon, where 'he humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers,' the LORD heard him, restored him to Jerusalem, and Manasseh then removed the idols and altars he had set up. His personal repentance was real and is one of Scripture's great testimonies that no sinner is beyond God's mercy. But Kings, writing the national history, records that the damage was done — the nation he had seduced did not truly turn, and the sentence on Judah stood.) The chapter is teaching us a profound double truth held between Kings and Chronicles: a man may sin terribly and still find personal mercy if he humbles himself (Manasseh repented and was forgiven), and yet the consequences of his influence on others may run on past his repentance (the nation he corrupted still went into exile). The chapter is teaching us that personal forgiveness, however complete, does not always undo the temporal and communal consequences of our sin — and that the most fearful legacy a leader can leave is not merely his own guilt but the corruption he has worked into the people who followed him. Manasseh found mercy; the Jerusalem he filled with idols and blood did not escape the judgment his reign had earned.

The chapter is teaching us that personal forgiveness, however complete, does not always undo the temporal and communal consequences of our sin — and that the most fearful legacy a leader can leave is not merely his own guilt but the corruption he has worked into the people who followed him. Manasseh found mercy; the Jerusalem he filled with idols and blood did not escape the judgment his reign had earned.

Amon was twenty and two years old when he began to reign, and he reigned two years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Meshullemeth, the daughter of Haruz of Jotbah. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as his father Manasseh did. And he walked in all the way that his father walked in, and served the idols that his father served, and worshipped them: And he forsook the LORD God of his fathers, and walked not in the way of the LORD. Guidance: Amon — evil without repentance. Manasseh's son Amon reigns just two years, and the verdict is bleak: 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as his father Manasseh did. And he walked in all the way that his father walked in, and served the idols that his father served... And he forsook the LORD God of his fathers.' Note what is conspicuously absent: Manasseh, for all his evil, finally repented (2 Chr 33); Amon imitated his father's SINS but not his father's late repentance. He took the worst of Manasseh and none of the eventual turning. The chapter is showing us the tragedy of inherited evil embraced without inherited repentance. Amon had before him both his father's wickedness and his father's eventual humbling before God; he chose to copy only the wickedness. The chapter is teaching us that children often imitate the visible patterns of their parents' lives more readily than the inner repentance — Amon 'served the idols that his father served' but did not humble himself as his father finally did. The chapter is teaching us the danger of growing up surrounded by idolatry: Amon knew nothing but Manasseh's altars and shrines, and he simply continued them, 'forsaking the LORD God of his fathers.' The chapter is teaching us that each generation must turn to the LORD for itself; a father's late repentance does not automatically transfer to the son, and a son who copies his father's sins while ignoring his father's repentance walks a short, dark road — as Amon's two-year reign and violent end will show.

And the servants of Amon conspired against him, and slew the king in his own house. And the people of the land slew all them that had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead. Now the rest of the acts of Amon which he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And he was buried in his sepulchre in the garden of Uzza: and Josiah his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Amon's assassination — and the dawn of Josiah. Amon's brief evil reign ends in violence: 'the servants of Amon conspired against him, and slew the king in his own house.' But then 'the people of the land' (the broader populace, perhaps the Davidic loyalists) executed the conspirators and 'made Josiah his son king in his stead.' The people preserved the Davidic succession against the coup. Amon is buried in the garden of Uzza like his father, and the chapter — and the long dark stretch from Ahaz through Manasseh and Amon — ends with the naming of the successor: 'Josiah his son reigned in his stead.' The chapter is closing on a note of hope amid the gathering doom. Just as the worst king Ahaz was followed by the great reformer Hezekiah (ch 18), now the wicked line of Manasseh and Amon gives way to Josiah — who will be Judah's last great king, the reformer who rediscovers the Book of the Law and leads the most thorough reformation in the nation's history (chs 22-23). The chapter is teaching us, once more, the LORD's faithfulness to the Davidic line through the darkest seasons: the people 'made Josiah his son king,' the lamp of David continues, and a child is set on the throne who will love the LORD with all his heart. The chapter is teaching us that even when judgment has been pronounced and is certain, the LORD still raises up His servants and gives His people seasons of grace and reform. The sentence on Judah stood (because of Manasseh's provocations), yet the LORD would still give Judah the gift of Josiah's reign — a final, glorious season of return before the end. The chapter is teaching us that God's pronounced judgments do not cancel His ongoing mercies; even on a people under sentence, He pours out the kindness of a godly king, calling them to repentance to the very end. The long descent through Ahaz, Manasseh, and Amon closes; the reformation of Josiah is about to dawn.

The chapter is teaching us, once more, the LORD's faithfulness to the Davidic line through the darkest seasons: the people 'made Josiah his son king,' the lamp of David continues, and a child is set on the throne who will love the LORD with all his heart. The chapter is teaching us that even when judgment has been pronounced and is certain, the LORD still raises up His servants and gives His people seasons of grace and reform. The sentence on Judah stood (because of Manasseh's provocations), yet the LORD would still give Judah the gift of Josiah's reign — a final, glorious season of return before the end. The chapter is teaching us that God's pronounced judgments do not cancel His ongoing mercies; even on a people under sentence, He pours out the kindness of a godly king, calling them to repentance to the very end. The long descent through Ahaz, Manasseh, and Amon closes; the reformation of Josiah is about to dawn.
